title: Simon from Cyrene part 2
scripture_text: ‘’A certain man from Cyrene, Simon, the father of Alexander and Rufus, was passing by on his way in from the country, and they forced him to carry the cross.’’
devotional:
There is a possibility that Simon may had been a Jewish pilgrim who had travelled from Cyrene to celebrate the passover. If this is the case, he was a man on a spiritual journey who desired to meet with God. He may have been looking forward to coming to the temple, possibly for the first time to make sacrifices in the temple. This may have been something he had looked forward to for a long time; but there was a problem. When he began to carry the cross of Jesus he would have been designated as being unclean, preventing him from going to the temple, from making sacrifices and excluded from the passover celebrations. As the cross was placed on his back he was no longer able to partake in his religious /customs and practices of the passover. He was separated from the ritualistic worship of his fore fathers through the cross of Jesus.

He would have been unable to fulfil his intended purpose for his visit to Jerusalem. Sometimes, when we begin to share in the sufferings of Jesus, our plans get thwarted, even good spiritual plans that we have earnestly made. I find this very challenging. Something in me would rather go to the temple with the masses, and have an easy ‘religion’ than carry a cross, I find it easier to hold onto my old religious ways than carry a defining cross behind Jesus and be viewed as an object of disdain and criminalised by my association with him. To carry the cross of Jesus would not have been seen as a badge honour but a symbol of profound shame. There was no visible glory in this act of sharing in Jesus’ suffering, but there was a profound identifying with him, that is only possible in suffering.

One final thought. The way of the cross is not for ‘super-hero’s.’ Jesus’ body had been robbed of its strength because of a long night of interrogation and brutal torture. As God he could have adorned the ‘cape of divinity,’ As the God man he could have made the journey to Galgotha in his own strength. But instead he chose in humility to embrace his humanity with all its weakness and pain. He allowed himself to need the help of another. This is still the way of the cross. We either can be a Simon or we need a Simon. To share in the sufferings of Jesus means that you become more than a passer-by.

Have you ever thought about the character of a passer by? A passer by is someone who makes little if any difference, they are people who take no concern or responsibility for those they are passing. Sadly it is too easy for anyone of us to live our lives as those who simply pass by. We keep our distance, we don’t get involved, we are too focused on our own journey. However, when we begin to share in the suffering of Jesus, we loose our status as those who simply pass by. We become those who will accept the cross so we can follow Jesus.

One of the greatest tragedies for any Christian is not having a cross to carry, or not knowing how to carry it, but, becoming someone who just passes by. To share in the suffering means that we become a part of the greatest and most essential work in the history of the world. This is the opposite to just passing by. The will of God is accomplished through those who have their present interrupted by the cross, and the future defined by the cross. Those who humbly accept the shameful, humiliating position of accepting the cross are those who are able to carry out God’s purposes because their lives are no longer about them.

We have all been given circumstances and burdens that we haven’t chosen for ourselves. Like Simon, our hopes and plans may have been destroyed. However these days may give us a unique opportunity to follow Jesus in ways that we have never done before and to share in His suffering so that we can experience the depth of fellowship with Him we all long for. As God’s story unfolds in these days, let us not become like those who just pass by but let us become people who are used by God to show his story to the world.
prayer: Father I confess it is so easy to embrace the status of a ‘passer by’ who does not get involved. Help me to willingly care for those around me.
html:
Simon from Cyrene part 2

Study Text: Mark 15:21
‘’A certain man from Cyrene, Simon, the father of Alexander and Rufus, was passing by on his way in from the country, and they forced him to carry the cross.’’

There is a possibility that Simon may had been a Jewish pilgrim who had travelled from Cyrene to celebrate the passover. If this is the case, he was a man on a spiritual journey who desired to meet with God. He may have been looking forward to coming to the temple, possibly for the first time to make sacrifices in the temple. This may have been something he had looked forward to for a long time; but there was a problem. When he began to carry the cross of Jesus he would have been designated as being unclean, preventing him from going to the temple, from making sacrifices and excluded from the passover celebrations. As the cross was placed on his back he was no longer able to partake in his religious /customs and practices of the passover. He was separated from the ritualistic worship of his fore fathers through the cross of Jesus.

He would have been unable to fulfil his intended purpose for his visit to Jerusalem. Sometimes, when we begin to share in the sufferings of Jesus, our plans get thwarted, even good spiritual plans that we have earnestly made. I find this very challenging. Something in me would rather go to the temple with the masses, and have an easy ‘religion’ than carry a cross, I find it easier to hold onto my old religious ways than carry a defining cross behind Jesus and be viewed as an object of disdain and criminalised by my association with him. To carry the cross of Jesus would not have been seen as a badge honour but a symbol of profound shame. There was no visible glory in this act of sharing in Jesus’ suffering, but there was a profound identifying with him, that is only possible in suffering.

One final thought. The way of the cross is not for ‘super-hero’s.’ Jesus’ body had been robbed of its strength because of a long night of interrogation and brutal torture. As God he could have adorned the ‘cape of divinity,’ As the God man he could have made the journey to Galgotha in his own strength. But instead he chose in humility to embrace his humanity with all its weakness and pain. He allowed himself to need the help of another. This is still the way of the cross. We either can be a Simon or we need a Simon. To share in the sufferings of Jesus means that you become more than a passer-by.

Have you ever thought about the character of a passer by? A passer by is someone who makes little if any difference, they are people who take no concern or responsibility for those they are passing. Sadly it is too easy for anyone of us to live our lives as those who simply pass by. We keep our distance, we don’t get involved, we are too focused on our own journey. However, when we begin to share in the suffering of Jesus, we loose our status as those who simply pass by. We become those who will accept the cross so we can follow Jesus.

One of the greatest tragedies for any Christian is not having a cross to carry, or not knowing how to carry it, but, becoming someone who just passes by. To share in the suffering means that we become a part of the greatest and most essential work in the history of the world. This is the opposite to just passing by. The will of God is accomplished through those who have their present interrupted by the cross, and the future defined by the cross. Those who humbly accept the shameful, humiliating position of accepting the cross are those who are able to carry out God’s purposes because their lives are no longer about them.

We have all been given circumstances and burdens that we haven’t chosen for ourselves. Like Simon, our hopes and plans may have been destroyed. However these days may give us a unique opportunity to follow Jesus in ways that we have never done before and to share in His suffering so that we can experience the depth of fellowship with Him we all long for. As God’s story unfolds in these days, let us not become like those who just pass by but let us become people who are used by God to show his story to the world.

Prayer: Father I confess it is so easy to embrace the status of a ‘passer by’ who does not get involved. Help me to willingly care for those around me.
Bible in a year reading plan: . Psalms 10-14
